text: The production of chemicals using renewable energy and carbon dioxide (CO2) is one of the key methods for mitigating climate change. However, the electrochemical reduction of CO2 into propylene, a valuable commodity chemical, is highly kinetically challenging because of the C-C bond coupling and 18-electron transfer involved. In this study, we show the first report of the electrosynthesis of propylene on copper nanocrystals (NCs) at a peak geometric current density of −5.5 mA cm−2. Our CuCl-derived copper NCs contain preferred (100) and (111) facets that greatly enhance the formation of key *C1 and *C2 species. A large decrease in propylene production is found when only carbon monoxide (CO) is used as reactant. Using the competitive isotope-labeling experiments between CO2 and CO, we infer that the rate-determining step of propylene synthesis is likely to be the coupling of either adsorbed or molecular CO2 (or carboxyl) species with *C2 species produced in the ethylene pathway.
